sections.title: Abstract — The Living Cinematic Civilization
sections.summary: Frames Cineora as a substrate that converts cinematic imagination into persistent, ownable, and economically alive civilizations. Introduces the four primitives (Living Scene NFTs, Agent Mesh, Seven Civilization Layers, $CINE) and restates the north star.

sections.title: 1. Introduction — From Content to Organism
sections.summary: Diagnoses the abundance paradox created by generative video and argues for continuity over production volume. Identifies the three 2026-era technical conditions (LLM/diffusion quality, WebGPU, Solana maturation) that make persistent on-chain worlds feasible now.

sections.title: 2. Market Analysis
sections.summary: Sizes the opportunity with cited sources: PwC Global Entertainment & Media Outlook ($2.8T → $3.4T), Goldman Sachs creator economy ($250B → $480B), Grand View Research AI in media ($14.8B → $99.5B), McKinsey GenAI ($2.6–4.4T), and Statista NFTs ($26.9B). Defines SAM and a plausible SOM of $250–750M annual protocol revenue.

sections.title: 3. Technology
sections.summary: Specifies the stack: React Three Fiber + Three.js + WebGPU for cinematic rendering, Anchor/Rust programs (cineora-core, cineora-living-scene, cineora-royalty, cineora-legacy), Metaplex Core extensions, Squads v4, Realms, Pyth + Switchboard oracles, Helius DAS indexing, and Solana state compression for high-volume scene metadata.

sections.title: 4. Architecture — The Seven Civilization Layers
sections.summary: Defines each of the seven layers — Aurora (rendered surface), Genesis (creation), Memory (continuity), Legacy (sovereignty), Cinema (distribution), Governance (coordination), Evolution (meta) — with its purpose, mechanic, and economic function. Anchors Aurora design tokens to the master-prompt color system.

sections.title: 5. Economics — $CINE
sections.summary: Defines $CINE's fixed 1B supply, allocation table (35% treasury, 18% community, 15% team, 12% grants, 8% public, 7% liquidity, 3% advisors, 2% reserve), the 5% protocol fee split 50/50 between burn and buyback, the four-year halving emissions schedule, and sustainability targets across 10/25/50-year horizons. Adds agent and civilization economic loops.

sections.title: 6. Governance
sections.summary: Specifies Creator DAO on Realms, per-world councils, and a 7-of-11 Squads multisig. Publishes the full proposal-threshold table (deposit, quorum, approval, timelock) by category, defines Reputation Token voting with 2% per-account cap and 10% annual decay, and constrains emergency guardian powers.

sections.title: 7. Security
sections.summary: Names the three tier-1 audit firms (Trail of Bits, OtterSec, Neodyme) with their program assignments, defines the Cantina-hosted bug bounty up to $500k, details agent safety controls, and specifies a SEV-1 through SEV-4 incident response framework with 14-day public disclosure.

sections.title: 8. Roadmap
sections.summary: Four phases with hard exit criteria: Genesis (Q3–Q4 2026, 50 worlds / 10k holders), Aurora (2027, 5k worlds / 250k MAU), Cinema (2028, 1M MAU / $25M revenue), Civilization (2029+, ten civilizations operating 36+ months under their original Legacy Constitution).

sections.title: 9. Risks
sections.summary: Categorizes risks honestly: regulatory (SEC Howey test, EU MiCA Article 6, OFAC screening), technical (smart contract exploits, oracle manipulation, agent hallucination, cryptographic obsolescence), market (creator-economy volatility, AI commoditization, concentration), and existential (the abundance paradox the protocol is built to address).

sections.title: 10. Future Research
sections.summary: Outlines four funded research threads: viewport-independent Cross-Reality Portals, consent-first Neural Narrative Interfaces, privacy-preserving Civilization Analytics, and Story Inheritance. Frames each as a multi-year research commitment rather than a launch promise.

sections.title: 11. Conclusion — The Cinematic Operating System of the Internet
sections.summary: Restates the north star and the category claim: Cineora is the layer beneath production, distribution, and monetization where stories become organisms. Closes the document on the permanence thesis — not what we generate, but what we choose to keep.
